root_cause: Catastrophic regex backtracking (ReDoS) in the WAF rule. The sreft-regex-svc container was running with a vulnerable WAF regex rule enabled. A load generator (sreft-cpu-load, 6 workers) sent crafted payloads (26 bytes) to the /check endpoint that triggered catastrophic backtracking in the regex, causing each evaluation to take 20+ seconds. This saturated all 4 worker goroutines, making the service unresponsive (99.77% CPU) while the process remained running. The sibling payments container (same image, WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1) was unaffected, confirming the WAF rule as the root cause.
actions_taken:
1. Stopped the load generator container (sreft-cpu-load) to halt the stream of requests triggering catastrophic backtracking.
2. Removed the stuck sreft-regex-svc container and recreated it with WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1 environment variable (matching the working sreft-cpu-payments instance configuration).
3. Verified the service is now responding on port 8080 with CPU usage at ~0.12% (down from 99.77%) and requests completing instantly.
postmortem: The regex-svc service experienced a Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) incident. The service was deployed with a vulnerable WAF regex rule enabled (logged as "WAF rule ENABLED (vulnerable)"). A load generator (sreft-cpu-load) sent 6 concurrent requests with 26-byte payloads to the /check endpoint, each triggering catastrophic backtracking in the regex pattern. Each regex evaluation took 20+ seconds, saturating all 4 worker goroutines and making the service completely unresponsive to health checks and user traffic. The sibling payments service (same image, WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1) was unaffected, confirming the WAF rule as the root cause. Remediation involved stopping the load generator and restarting the service with the WAF rule disabled. Long-term recommendations: (1) Fix the vulnerable regex pattern to avoid backtracking, (2) Add regex evaluation timeouts, (3) Implement request rate limiting, (4) Add CPU-based alerting to detect saturation earlier.
